perturbation.original_text: All of the confidential information (hereinafter "Confidential Information"), including, without limitation, all information relating to business plans, financial or technical matters, trade secrets, designs, know-how, inventions, operations and any other information received or acquired by one party ("Receiving Party") from the other ("Disclosing Party") in the course of exploring the possible business relationship shall be in written form and marked "Confidential," with the name of the Disclosing Party and the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: All of the confidential information (hereinafter "Proprietary Information"), including, without limitation, all information relating to business plans, financial or technical matters, trade secrets, designs, know-how, inventions, operations and any other information received or acquired by one party ("Receiving Party") from the other ("Disclosing Party") in the course of exploring the possible business relationship shall be in written form and marked "Confidential," with the name of the Disclosing Party and the date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the defined term from 'Confidential Information' to 'Proprietary Information' but still referring to it as 'Confidential' later in the same sentence creates an in-text contradiction. This will create confusion because the contract later in the document keeps referring to 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All of the confidential information (hereinafter "Proprietary Information"), including, without limitation, all information relating to business plans, financial or technical matters, trade secrets, designs, know-how, inventions, operations and any other information received or acquired by one party ("Receiving Party") from the other ("Disclosing Party") in the course of exploring the possible business relationship shall be in written form and marked "Confidential," with the name of the Disclosing Party and the date of disclosure. If the Confidential Information is initially disclosed orally, it shall be reduced to written form by the Disclosing Party (including the date of the oral disclosure and name of the Disclosing Party) and presented or mailed to the Receiving Party within fifteen (15) days of the first oral disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines 'Confidential Information' as 'Proprietary Information' but continues to use the term 'Confidential' in the same sentence, which contradicts the defined term. The contradicted text also uses both terms, creating confusion and ambiguity about which term refers to the information that needs to be kept private. This misalignment in terminology, as defined in the Perturbation Definitions, leads to an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: If the Confidential Information is initially disclosed orally, it shall be reduced to written form by the Disclosing Party (including the date of the oral disclosure and name of the Disclosing Party) and presented or mailed to the Receiving Party within fifteen (15) days of the first oral disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Confidential Data is initially disclosed orally, it shall be reduced to written form by the Disclosing Party (including the date of the oral disclosure and name of the Disclosing Party) and presented or mailed to the Receiving Party within fifteen (15) days of the first oral disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the phrase 'Confidential Information' to 'Confidential Data' in this section introduces an inconsistency. 'Confidential Information' is the defined term earlier, and this shift makes it unclear whether 'Confidential Data' falls under the same obligations. This is contradicting the intro paragraph that defined the term for the entire agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All of the confidential information (hereinafter "Confidential Information"), including, without limitation, all information relating to business plans, financial or technical matters, trade secrets, designs, know-how, inventions, operations and any other information received or acquired by one party ("Receiving Party") from the other ("Disclosing Party") in the course of exploring the possible business relationship shall be in written form and marked "Confidential," with the name of the Disclosing Party and the date of disclosure. If the Confidential Data is initially disclosed orally, it shall be reduced to written form by the Disclosing Party (including the date of the oral disclosure and name of the Disclosing Party) and presented or mailed to the Receiving Party within fifteen (15) days of the first oral disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The original text uses the defined term 'Confidential Information,' consistent with the contradicted text's definition. The changed text replaces this with 'Confidential Data,' which is undefined. This creates a contradiction because the contradicted text refers specifically to 'Confidential Information' and the change introduces uncertainty about whether the obligations apply to 'Confidential Data,' as per the explanation provided and adhering to the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: (b) The Confidential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) The Private Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Private Information' creates confusion about what information is actually protected under the agreement. Since 'Private Information' is never formally defined, it's unclear whether the obligations relating to 'Confidential Information' extend to it. This will contradict the sections that refer to 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All of the confidential information (hereinafter "Confidential Information"), including, without limitation, all information relating to business plans, financial or technical matters, trade secrets, designs, know-how, inventions, operations and any other information received or acquired by one party ("Receiving Party") from the other ("Disclosing Party") in the course of exploring the possible business relationship shall be in written form and marked "Confidential," with the name of the Disclosing Party and the date of disclosure. If the Confidential Information is initially disclosed orally, it shall be reduced to written form by the Disclosing Party (including the date of the oral disclosure and name of the Disclosing Party) and presented or mailed to the Receiving Party within fifteen (15) days of the first oral disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Private Information' creates a direct conflict with the 'Contradicted Text' section. The contradicted text explicitly defines and uses 'Confidential Information' and provides details on how it is to be treated (written form, marked 'Confidential', etc.). By changing the term to 'Private Information,' which is undefined in the contract, the section referencing 'Confidential Information' is now unclear and contradictory, as it's uncertain whether it applies to 'Private Information' as well. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition because the contract is now using multiple terms (Confidential Information and Private Information) seemingly interchangeably without a clear definition, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: All information disclosed which is not marked "Confidential," or not reduced to written form and marked "Confidential" if initially disclosed orally shall be considered to be non-confidential and shall not be subject to the obligations imposed by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: All information disclosed which is not marked "Proprietary," or not reduced to written form and marked "Confidential" if initially disclosed orally shall be considered to be non-confidential and shall not be subject to the obligations imposed by this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This creates a contradiction. The agreement states that information not marked 'Confidential' is non-confidential, however in the first portion of the contract, we said that 'Confidential Information' should be considered to be 'Proprietary Information'. This creates an in text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All of the confidential information (hereinafter "Confidential Information"), including, without limitation, all information relating to business plans, financial or technical matters, trade secrets, designs, know-how, inventions, operations and any other information received or acquired by one party ("Receiving Party") from the other ("Disclosing Party") in the course of exploring the possible business relationship shall be in written form and marked "Confidential," with the name of the Disclosing Party and the date of disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Confidential' with 'Proprietary' in the phrase 'not marked "Proprietary." The contradicted text requires that confidential information be marked 'Confidential'. The explanation states that 'Confidential Information' should be considered 'Proprietary Information' elsewhere in the contract. Thus, information not marked Proprietary is considered non-confidential, which conflicts with the section stating that Confidential information must be marked 'Confidential', creating a strong in-text contradiction as per the Misaligned Terminology definition.

perturbation.original_text: The existence and terms of this Agreement shall be treated as Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The existence and terms of this Agreement shall be treated as Protected Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Protected Information' here, without defining 'Protected Information' within the context of the agreement, creates uncertainty. Is 'Protected Information' subject to the same confidentiality requirements as 'Confidential Information'? The document now has contradicting directions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party shall: (a) hold the Confidential Information in confidence and not disclose it to third parties, except in the limited cases referred to in paragraph "6"; and (b) not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than exploring or examining the possibility of a business relationship between the parties.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Protected Information', without defining 'Protected Information', creates a contradiction. The contradicted text specifies obligations related to 'Confidential Information'. Because 'Protected Information' is undefined, it is unclear whether the same obligations apply, thus contradicting the original intent and creating uncertainty in the contract. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition, as the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall: (a) hold the Confidential Information in confidence and not disclose it to third parties, except in the limited cases referred to in paragraph "6"; and (b) not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than exploring or examining the possibility of a business relationship between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The Designated Recipient shall: (a) hold the Confidential Information in confidence and not disclose it to third parties, except in the limited cases referred to in paragraph "6"; and (b) not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than exploring or examining the possibility of a business relationship between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the defined role name of 'Receiving Party' to 'Designated Recipient' within this section introduces an inconsistency. 'Receiving Party' is used multiple times in the contract as being the party that receives confidential information, this shift makes it unclear whether 'Designated Recipient' now takes on the responsibilities that are defined to the 'Receiving Party', especially since the contract is still referring to 'Confidential Information' being the subject. This is contradicting the intro paragraph that defined the term for the entire agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All of the confidential information (hereinafter "Confidential Information"), including, without limitation, all information relating to business plans, financial or technical matters, trade secrets, designs, know-how, inventions, operations and any other information received or acquired by one party ("Receiving Party") from the other ("Disclosing Party") in the course of exploring the possible business relationship shall be in written form and marked "Confidential," with the name of the Disclosing Party and the date of disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Receiving Party' to 'Designated Recipient' creates a contradiction because the original contract defines 'Receiving Party' as the entity receiving confidential information. The 'Contradicted Text' clearly states that the term 'Receiving Party' applies to the party receiving confidential information from the 'Disclosing Party'. By changing the term in the Changed Text while still referring to 'Confidential Information,' it introduces ambiguity and contradicts the established definition, leading to uncertainty about which party is responsible for the obligations associated with receiving confidential information, thus fitting the definition of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction.
